era_context: The late 17th century: European powers consolidating colonial empires; Louis XIV at the height of French power; the Ottoman Empire in decline after the failed siege of Vienna (1683); the Qing Dynasty under Kangxi consolidating control over China; the Mughal Empire in India under Aurangzeb in its final decades.

events.title: Isaac Newton Publishes Principia Mathematica
events.description: On 5 July 1687, Isaac Newton's Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica (Mathematical Principles of Natural Philosophy), commonly known as the Principia, was published by the Royal Society in London. The work presented Newton's laws of motion and universal gravitation, providing a mathematical framework for understanding the physical universe. The Principia is considered one of the most influential scientific works ever published and marked the beginning of the Scientific Revolution's mature phase. It established Newton as the preeminent natural philosopher of the age and provided the foundation for classical mechanics.
events.certainty_note: The publication date and contents of the Principia are precisely documented.

events.title: Ottoman Sultan Mehmed IV Deposed; Süleyman II Becomes Sultan
events.description: In November 1687, the Ottoman Sultan Mehmed IV was deposed by the Janissary corps and replaced by his brother Süleyman II. The deposition reflected the Ottoman military's dissatisfaction with Mehmed IV's leadership, particularly following military defeats in the Morean War and the failed siege of Vienna (1683). Süleyman II's reign (1687–1691) was marked by continued military struggles and the empire's gradual loss of territory to European powers. The deposition illustrated the power of the Janissaries in Ottoman politics and the empire's internal instability.
events.certainty_note: The deposition of Mehmed IV and accession of Süleyman II are well-documented in Ottoman and European sources.

events.title: Aurangzeb Captures Golconda Fort
events.description: In September 1687, the Mughal Emperor Aurangzeb's forces captured the fort of Golconda, one of the last independent sultanates in the Deccan. The capture of Golconda, along with the earlier conquest of Bijapur (1686), brought most of the Deccan under direct Mughal control. However, the conquest was costly and did not eliminate Maratha resistance. The expansion of Mughal territory in the Deccan was ultimately unsustainable and contributed to the empire's overextension and decline.
key_figures: Abul Hasan Qutb Shah (Sultan of Golconda)

sources.name: Mughal court records and official histories

events.certainty_note: The capture of Golconda is well-documented in Mughal records.

events.title: Kangxi Emperor Consolidates Power in Mongolia
events.description: By 1687, the Kangxi Emperor had successfully consolidated Qing control over Mongolia, defeating the Khalkha Mongols and their allies. The Qing victory at the Battle of Ulan Butung (1690) was preceded by diplomatic and military preparations in 1687. The consolidation of Mongolia under Qing rule was a major achievement that expanded the Qing Empire's territorial control and secured its northern frontier. This expansion made the Qing the dominant power in East Asia.

sources.name: Qing imperial records and court diaries

events.certainty_note: The consolidation of Qing control over Mongolia is documented in imperial records, though specific events in 1687 are less detailed than those of the 1690 battle.

events.title: Louis XIV Revokes the Edict of Nantes (Aftermath and Enforcement)
events.description: In 1685, Louis XIV had revoked the Edict of Nantes, which had granted religious toleration to French Huguenots (Protestants). By 1687, the revocation was being actively enforced, leading to the persecution of Huguenots and the forced conversion of many to Catholicism. Thousands of Huguenots fled France, seeking refuge in Protestant countries such as England, the Dutch Republic, and Prussia. The revocation and its enforcement had significant economic and demographic consequences for France, as many skilled artisans and merchants left the country.
events.certainty_note: The enforcement of the revocation of the Edict of Nantes is well-documented in French royal records and refugee accounts.

events.title: Glorious Revolution Preparations: William III Mobilizes Dutch Forces
events.description: In 1687, William III of Orange, Stadtholder of the Dutch Republic, began preparations for an invasion of England to depose his father-in-law, King James II. William mobilized Dutch military forces and began diplomatic negotiations with English nobles and politicians who opposed James II's pro-Catholic policies. The preparations culminated in William's invasion of England in November 1688. The year 1687 thus marks the beginning of the political and military maneuvers that would lead to the Glorious Revolution.

sources.name: Dutch state papers and William's correspondence

events.certainty_note: The preparations for the Glorious Revolution are documented in diplomatic correspondence, though the exact timeline of events in 1687 is somewhat uncertain.

events.title: Venetian-Ottoman Naval Campaigns in the Aegean
events.description: In 1687, Venetian and Ottoman naval forces engaged in campaigns in the Aegean Sea as part of the Morean War (1684–1699). Venetian forces achieved several naval victories, capturing islands and coastal fortifications from the Ottomans. The naval campaigns reflected Venice's continued naval power and the Ottoman Empire's declining maritime capacity. The campaigns were part of the broader European-Ottoman conflict that characterized the late 17th century.

sources.name: Venetian state papers and naval records

events.certainty_note: The naval campaigns are documented in Venetian and Ottoman records, though specific details of individual engagements may be uncertain.

disconfirming_evidence: Newton's Principia is often celebrated as a triumph of pure reason and empirical science, but modern scholarship emphasizes that Newton's work was deeply embedded in theological and metaphysical concerns. Newton himself was an alchemist and theologian, and his physics was intended to reveal divine order. Additionally, the Principia's reception was not immediate or universal; many natural philosophers remained skeptical of Newton's gravitational theory for decades. The revocation of the Edict of Nantes is sometimes presented as a straightforward act of religious intolerance, but recent scholarship emphasizes that Louis XIV's motivations were complex, involving both religious conviction and political calculations about national unity. The Huguenot exodus, while significant, did not have the catastrophic economic impact on France that was once claimed.
historiographic_note: 1687 is well-documented in European sources, particularly English, French, Dutch, and Italian records. The publication of Newton's Principia dominates the scientific historical narrative, reflecting the bias of Western historiography toward European intellectual achievements. Asian sources (Chinese, Ottoman, Indian) are available but are often underutilized. The perspective is heavily weighted toward elite political, military, and intellectual events; the experiences of common people, women, and non-European populations are largely absent. The Huguenot perspective on the revocation of the Edict of Nantes is well-represented in refugee accounts, but indigenous and non-European perspectives are largely absent.

graph_edges.note: Newton's Principia was published in the same year that William III was preparing for the Glorious Revolution
The deposition of Mehmed IV and accession of Süleyman II led to continued Ottoman military struggles in the Morean War
The capture of Golconda contributed to Aurangzeb's overextension, which led to the empire's decline after his death in 1707
William III's preparations in 1687 led directly to the Glorious Revolution in 1688
